rounds.blurb: Buzz Aldrin on the Sea of Tranquility, photographed by Neil Armstrong during Apollo 11.
rounds.story: Armstrong carried the camera for nearly the whole moonwalk, which is why the mission's most famous portrait is of Aldrin — Armstrong appears only as a reflection in the gold visor. The pair spent 21 hours on the Sea of Tranquility.

rounds.blurb: Mount St. Helens erupts on the morning of May 18 — the deadliest volcanic event in U.S. history.
rounds.story: The eruption removed the top 1,300 feet of the mountain, flattened 230 square miles of forest, and killed 57 people. USGS photographer Austin Post made this frame from a light plane as the column climbed 15 miles.

rounds.blurb: Opening ceremony of the Summer Olympics at the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum.
rounds.story: Los Angeles staged the first privately financed Games — and turned a surplus north of $200 million while fourteen Eastern Bloc nations stayed home. The ceremony opened with a jet-pack flight and eighty-four grand pianos playing Rhapsody in Blue.

rounds.blurb: Times Square in high summer, photographed for the EPA's Documerica project.
rounds.credit: Dan McCoy / EPA Documerica, National Archives — public domain
rounds.story: Dan McCoy shot Times Square for Documerica, an EPA project that dispatched photographers to record American life in the early 1970s. The district was sliding into its gritty pre-cleanup era of grindhouses and peep shows.

rounds.blurb: Residents of Sacramento Street watch the fires after the San Francisco earthquake.
rounds.story: The magnitude 7.9 quake and the fires that followed destroyed most of San Francisco and left over half the city homeless. Genthe shot this with a borrowed camera — his own studio and equipment were already burning.
